entries.title: Is the Age of Reading Over?
entries.summary: Rose Horowitch on her cover story and what happens when a society becomes “postliterate”
entries.content:
Rose Horowitch on her cover story and what happens when a society becomes “postliterate”

Subscribe here: Apple Podcasts | Spotify | YouTube | Overcast | Pocket Casts

Over the past 18 months, scholars have debated—and likely will continue to long into the future—why voters returned Donald Trump to the presidency. One intriguing theory: It’s because Americans don’t read books anymore. Fewer than half of all adults reported reading a book of any kind in 2022; only 38 percent read a novel or short story. And the proportion of Americans reading for pleasure on any given day is plummeting, according to the latest Atlantic cover story, “The End of Reading Is Here,” by Rose Horowitch.



We are all awash in text, of course: messages from friends, headlines, captions on videos. But not the kinds of rich, layered sentences that have shaped our brains for centuries and given rise to many academic disciplines. Hence, we are a populace susceptible to a man who can capture attention, stir emotion, and breezily contradict himself, because his words are not etched in stone, or anywhere, really.

Horowitch joins Radio Atlantic to discuss what it means to live in a postliterate society, what we’ve lost, and how we might recover.

The following is a transcript of the episode:

Hanna Rosin: We’re going to have a conversation about the end of reading today. This idea, proposed in the most recent Atlantic cover story, is that the age of reading is turning out to be a short anomaly in human history and that we are fast entering a postliterate society.

But before we do that, I want you to think about the arc of your own relationship with books. How did you read as a kid? In school? When did that change? Did you notice it change? What’s the last book you read, absorbed, and enjoyed?

[Music]

Rosin: I’m Hanna Rosin. This is Radio Atlantic.

I’m guessing that you’ve heard someone lament about how students—in middle school, high school, or even in college—don’t read whole books anymore. Maybe you have a teacher friend who complains about having to assign excerpts or increasingly simple texts. Maybe you are a young student, and books seem annoying and time-consuming to you. Or maybe you’re an adult and just find yourself trying to read but instead reaching for your phone every couple of pages.

The argument we’re discussing today is much broader than mere phone distraction and spans back to ancient Egypt. It’s about how reading shaped the human mind and then human society, and how that’s now veering in a different direction.

Rosin: Do you think more people actually read your full article or heard about the debate on TikTok?

Rose Horowitch: I hope that people read it, but I don’t know.

Rosin: I’m talking today to the author of that cover story, Rose Horowitch, who is not the elderly professor type that you might normally assume wrote an Atlantic story called “The End of Reading Is Here.”

Horowitch: It’s so funny because I initially had a joke in there, like, You probably think I’m retirement age, reading this whole piece.

Rosin: Rose is actually in her 20s.

Horowitch: Yes.

Rosin: She did grow up learning to love to read. But then as she got older, she found it harder.

Horowitch: That’s a story probably a lot of people can relate to, of reading much more than when you were young and then gradually noticing it disappear.

I think I was noticing more so when I would try to read a book that I would just, after every couple pages, want to check my phone in case I missed a notification. Or I would take a look at my watch to make sure that I didn’t have any notifications. So I had to start putting my phone in drawers or putting it in another room so that I really could not check it. So that was more what I noticed, yeah.

Rosin: Right. So that’s your experience, and from your article, I’m seeing that’s the experience of lots of people. So just lay out the basics. In what ways and among whom has reading books declined?

Horowitch: So reading books has declined very broadly, even among the groups of people that, you know, have been the most stalwart readers: Women and retirees and college graduates, they’re seeing a decline. We know that in the past two decades, the share of Americans that read a book or an article on any given day has declined by 40 percent.

And that, as of 2022, which is the last year that we have really great data for, fewer than half of all Americans read a book at all in the year, and only 38 percent read a novel or short story. So we know that reading is just really in decline kind of across demographic groups.

Rosin: And is it the types of books people now read, has that changed as well? I’m feeling very superior now ’cause I just finished my book last night, but anyway, keep going. (Laughs.)

Horowitch: (Laughs.)

Rosin: Yeah.

Horowitch: So the types of books—we know that when people do read, the books that they’re reading are shorter and simpler than they used to be. New York Times best sellers have sentences that are about one-third shorter than they were a century ago. And I spoke with the head of the New York Public Library, and he was saying that young-adult fiction has really become one of their most popular offerings, even among people who are not young adults. So we’re definitely seeing that when people do pick up a book, the book that they read is shorter and the sentences are simpler and the vocabulary’s simpler, and that’s a real change as well.

Rosin: You’ve written a lot about the decline of reading. I feel like it’s your subbeat now. (Laughs.)

Horowitch: Yes.

Rosin: When you were doing research for this particular story, was there statistics or a comment from a teacher or something that really stuck with you or surprised you?

Horowitch: I think some of the things that have been most chilling for me in reporting this out were the things that were really about children reading. And so we know that only 2 percent of American adults read to a child on any given day. And we do see that kind of every year older a child gets, the less they like to read.

I spoke with one researcher who did focus groups, and high-school students described reading for pleasure as an alien practice. And so that really stuck out in my mind, just like, Okay, how did we get to this point, when kids view reading this way?

Rosin: The way this argument is framed—and you just used the word chilling—is: There’s a problem. Who should we blame for the problem? Or a lot of people will read the article that way, and some will immediately go to Kids today, they’re always on their phones, and this generation of students. Some will say schools just gave up, and it’s this iteration of education that’s the problem.

Do you, in your head, have a clear answer for who to blame?

Horowitch: Well, it’s difficult to know exactly how much each factor plays into it. I definitely don’t think that it’s just kids these days.

Rosin: That’s why I’m glad you wrote this story—

Horowitch: Right.

Rosin: —and not the retiring professor. Right.

Horowitch: Yes. As a member of Gen Z, I’m like, If it’s kids these days, it’s me. But I think this is a story of technologies crowding out the time that people would’ve once spent reading books. We know that when the television was first invented, American households were spending tons of time watching that. Then obviously streaming, internet, smartphone, social media—it all just takes up so much of our time now that might have previously been devoted to reading a book.

Rosin: We’re in some ways inundated with text. I feel on a daily basis I read a much higher quantity of actual words than I used to. So what is the difference?

Horowitch: Yes, exactly. So people often talk about this as a literacy crisis, and I don’t think that that’s right at all. Our literacy rate is historically very high. We are probably reading more words than ever with social media, the number of emails you get for your job, and text messages with friends and everything.

And so the difference is that we’re almost swimming in so much text that we just don’t actually have the time or, sort of, attention to kind of focus for an extended period on a rich or complex work, because our attention is turning to so many different things. And so that’s why I called it really a postliterate age instead of an illiterate age.

Rosin: Explain postliterate. That’s a really important word in your argument. What does postliterate mean?

Horowitch: Right. So what I was trying to get across is that reading is not just one mode of communication or one skill among many. When reading and writing first appeared about 6,000 years ago, it transformed society, and it changed the way people thought and politics and culture. And so I was trying to get across that I think that we’re living through a civilizational shift on that scale, where we’re gonna see changes in our consciousness, changes in the candidates we elect, and what our culture values, and even how we tell the story of our civilization.

So that was what I was trying to get across by postliterate, that we can still read, but it’s an age where text is just not the kind of medium that we use to spread information or knowledge or culture or entertainment, and that that will have really far-reaching effects.

Rosin: That was an excellent and fast summary, but I also feel like one thing about this argument is: It can seem like an elite argument that you and I and the people who read The Atlantic care about. It’s sort of ironic that you write—I don’t know how many words it was—a 7,000-word, very-well-written piece to explain how your generation doesn’t really read or have an attention span anymore.

So I actually wanna dig in a little more slowly and deeply to why reading is important, ’cause we can summarize it quickly and just say to people reading is important. But I think until you actually understand the shifts that happened in human evolution or our brain via reading, it doesn’t seem that important. I can be like, I read what I want. Leave me alone.

So maybe we’ll start with the work of Walter Ong.

Walter Ong: Now, I understand that my task is to try to talk to you. Would you mind if I took my jacket off? It’s a little bit warmer in here—

Rosin: I love his work because he’s a priest, so everything he writes about and thinks about has a spiritual dimension. He was also a teacher. So talk about his work around oral traditions versus written traditions and how they change the brain.

Horowitch: Right. So I think the one thing that Ong really understood was that writing text is just a very different process than speaking it.

Ong: Writing has reinforced the old. It’s made talkers talk more, but it’s made them talk differently.

[Music]

Horowitch: We tend to use more complex sentence structures and vocabulary. When you write something down, it gives you distance because you can see it reflected back at you. So you can go back to it and get new understanding. You can kind of take on a more complex analytical task. It’s a more dispassionate spread of information because you don’t immediately see the person who wrote it at the same time.

Ong: It was the first time it ever occurred to him that every one of those definitions in the dictionary was something that somebody said this word means. The word didn’t come equipped with it. Somebody had to figure that out and say, This is what it means.

Horowitch: And so he was arguing that it’s just very different and that this ushers in a sort of entirely new mode of thinking, a kind of rational, linear, analytical way of thinking. And that that is a precondition for a lot of the modern types of study that we think about today. Philosophy and modern science and history as an academic discipline, that those things required reading and writing.

Ong: They can learn facts, but they cannot learn how to think in the way in which you have to think. The thought patterns that you have to be able to use cannot be mastered without writing.

Rosin: Yeah, there’s this quote you reference from Amusing Ourselves to Death, the 1985 Neil Postman book: “Writing freezes speech.” And I think what he meant by that is that speech happens fluidly, but writing inserts a pause; and that pause allows us to reflect, shape our thoughts; and all this metacognition is happening when you write that doesn’t necessarily happen in speech, because speech is constantly moving. Is it something like that? Because I never actually thought in detail about what reading does to your brain versus writing.

Horowitch: Yeah, so I think there’s two elements to it. One is that we just speak a lot faster than we write. And so it forces you to slow down and think a little bit more about what you’re putting out there. And then at the same time, when it is frozen before you, you can see it reflected back at you, this message, and so you can return to it for new understanding, think about how to reorder it, edit it, all these things that we can’t really do with speech, ’cause it just disappears into the ether.

And so it sort of allows for this kind of logical way of thinking, and that, yeah, that quote from Postman really stuck out to me too.

Rosin: I’m just realizing that podcasting, therefore, is for Neanderthals because— (Laughs.)

Horowitch: No. No, because it’s secondary orality. It’s mediated through this kind of writing, ’cause we’re talking about writing.

Rosin: Okay. Secondary orality. I’ll take it.

Okay, so that’s the long period that we’ve been through now, where we’ve evolved our brains, evolved a lot of human intellectual disciplines via reading, and now we’re entering towards postliteracy. What are the characteristics of a postliterate society, as opposed to the literate society you just explained?

Horowitch: Yeah, so as part of my reporting for this piece, I interviewed a lot of cognitive scientists and was kind of trying to understand, Okay, what does happen to our consciousness? And I think what we see is that we know from neuroscience that what you practice is what your brain gets good at. And so as we’re reading less, we’re going to have less background knowledge to aid in comprehension. We’re going to have a less rational, analytical mode of thinking.

We’re more accustomed to switching our attention and less accustomed to a kind of linear, extended focus on something. Even two decades ago, the average attention span on a screen was two-and-a-half minutes. And five years ago, it was 47 seconds.

Rosin: There are some times when you describe it dispassionately, it almost sounds like a superpower, you know? Because speed is often thought of as good. We test students on how quickly they can do things. Tests are always timed. So I don’t know, just as you were talking, I was thinking, Could speed be some kind of intellectual superpower that we just don’t understand yet?

Horowitch: Right. Well, I was trying to leave open that possibility. We do see, even as we see on tests, that it appears that IQs have been declining for the past two decades. At the same time, we are doing better on some forms of spatial rotation. So I definitely wanted to leave open the possibility that there could be these cognitive benefits that we just don’t understand and that this is a trade-off, but what we get will end up being worth it. And I think it’s too soon to tell if that’s possible.

[Music]

Rosin: After the break, who might thrive in a postliterate society? A clue: He’s currently president.

[Break]

Rosin: Okay, so that’s on the level of, like, an individual brain. There’s also an entire civilization because you start—

Horowitch: (Laughs.)

Rosin: (Laughs.) —the story at a broad civilization level. We can start at the easiest place, is with Trump. You describe him as the ideal president for a postliterate society. What does that mean?

Horowitch: Yeah, so what I meant by that was that it’s sort of difficult to imagine Trump being elected in a country where information is primarily spread through text. But he really understands how to kind of grab attention and keep it in this postliterate age.

So we see that he actually has kind of retrieved a lot of conventions from preliterate times. He contradicts himself as though there’s no record of what he said before.

[Music]

President Trump (in May): You’ll never have a leak. It’s very strong. If you had a knife, you can’t even cut it, it’s so strong, so powerful. It’s like powerful rubber.

Reporter (in June): National Guard and police have been all over the Mall. How would these vandals have gotten so close to do something like that?

Trump (in June): We didn’t have a lot of them then. Who would think that somebody would go into a pool and take a knife and start cutting it?

Horowitch: He speaks with a lot of emotion, you know, often anger, and that’s just easier to remember than when someone gives a sort of dispassionate speech.

Trump: I think it’s a disgrace. And I say that, and I say that, and that’s something that Nazi Germany would have done and did do. I think it’s a disgrace.

Horowitch: We also see that he uses these kind of epithets that almost call back to Homer in the way that the poet described people. He says, “Sleepy Joe,” “Crooked Hillary,” and that’s just way easier to remember and repeat. And you just start to associate it.

Trump: I call him Crooked Joe and Sleepy Joe. I find them to be equal actually. I do polls. Which is better: Crooked Joe? Or Sleepy Joe? And they poll about even.

Horowitch: So we see that Trump has kind of understood how to entertain people, go viral, present things as very simple and authentic, and that’s helped him get elected.

Rosin: The piece you wrote caused quite a stir, and especially among people who love books.

People who love books tend to read The Atlantic, so maybe we’re getting a skewed sample. But let’s just say it caused quite a stir in the book-reading public, especially the publishing world. Is there any pushback that you’ve received that you think is interesting?

Horowitch: Well, I think it’s interesting—there are a lot of positive signs for reading, which I did try to indicate in the story. Print-book sales were higher last year than they were a decade ago, and so that is certainly a fair critique.

But at the same time, I tried to indicate in the piece, when I noted that, that what we’re seeing is that the people who are devoted to text are perhaps even more devoted to it than ever before. They spend more time reading than they used to, but they’re just becoming a smaller and smaller minority in society.

Rosin: One thing I was thinking about is the democratization of text. When you wrote about people reading shorter sentences or YA novels, maybe one way of looking at this is that there used to be gatekeepers, people who would tell us what was literary, what was considered good to read, whereas now maybe you read what you want, which might be Colleen Hoover, which might be fan fiction, which might be someone’s online manifesto. And is that necessarily bad in the same way everything’s been democratized? Because if more people are buying books, it just means maybe they’re buying not literary books, but books.

Horowitch: No, and that—I think that is a fair critique. And it sort of fits in with audiobooks almost, that they make it more accessible to read. And so I definitely do think it is a good thing if stories and information has been democratized and are accessible to more people. I think the challenge is just: Even as these things have become more widely available, we are still seeing a huge decline in reading. So it’s like, have people taken advantage of them? It’s not clear to me.

Rosin: Yeah. And is your worry that because you write so much about reading inside the educational system, that down the road people will even stop reading those kinds of books because reading is just so much less fluidly integrated into education at all levels?

Horowitch: Definitely we see in schools that teachers are just assigning far fewer books. I think a survey from last year found that most middle- and high-school teachers assign between zero and four books a year. And so we do see a big change where there’s a lot more emphasis on excerpts and on reading-comprehension drills.

I guess my concern would be: You want everyone to have the option to read any work that they would want to, and so just making sure that students are equipped with the skills and mode of attention and practice that they can read whatever they want to instead of having some of it be unavailable to them.

Rosin: Right. And I suppose there’s also a return to the analog, like reading retreats and that kind of thing. So there’s some kind of self-conscious niche, like I’m a reader. So it’s almost like your identity is you are a reader, and you go out to a reading club, and that’s who you are. It’s not everybody. It’s not a sign of a literate society. But it is your people.

Horowitch: Yeah. One thing that really struck me in that Postman book was that before radio and television was invented, there was no way to be a reader, because there was no way to not be a reader. If you knew how to read, you had to. That was the only way to get information.

And so it’s only now that being a reader is optional, that it really can be an identity marker. And we are seeing with some young people that there really is this drive towards more analog technologies. It seems to be a backlash to living in a digital age where everything feels ephemeral.

I spoke with the head of the Cleveland Public Library, and he was saying that the young people that come in, they want a print book because they want something that feels tangible and concrete and that they can hold. So there certainly is that countervailing sign.

Rosin: I know. It’s so funny. It’s like a print book is like a vinyl record.

Horowitch: (Laughs.)

Rosin: (Laughs.) Like, there will be stores that are like, We sell books and vinyl records.

The bombshell of AI: One thing I noticed is that AI has an oddly formal way of speaking to you. Like, it’s a lot more like the letters you quoted from the average Civil War soldier than it is like texting. And so I know that, superficially, it seems like, Of course AI will destroy deep thinking and reading, but I also sense that AI returns a level of kind of full-sentences formality. Like, Here is how you should think through things. There is a kind of a return to discipline and what it’s presenting to you in a way that I find interesting.

Horowitch: No, it’s funny. It’s sort of the ultimate irony that we talk about this technology that’s just gonna destroy thinking or whatever, and it’s text or, at least, now it communicates with us via text. And so the point that I tried to make is that we are actually living in a time where there’s more text.

AI has led to a threefold increase in the number of books released on Amazon, more scientific papers being published. And a lot of this text at first brush appears polished and professional and something that you might trust and take in. And so that’s why I think it’s more important than ever to cultivate your powers of critical thinking and discernment, because AI is creating this superabundance of text, and we need to be able to sift through it.

Rosin: Maybe that’s a useful way to think about this argument, that it’s not just about books. It’s not just about novels. It is about taking note of a world in which, because you are inundated with text, you can have this feeling that you are, in fact, reading a lot, but maybe pay attention to the ways in which whatever reading you’re doing is eroding an ability to think critically, think deeply, think independently. Just note that evolution or change.

[Music]

Horowitch: No, and I think that that has been true for so long. But one thing that I think we might be seeing now is that for a while at least, a lot of information on social media was presented in text. And now we sort of see with new capabilities just so much more short-form video.

TikToker 1: The United States of America is becoming postliterate.

TikToker 2: The end of reading is here, so I wanna talk about why this is a reality that I refuse to accept. Over the weekend, I read The Atlantic’s August cover story, about the death of reading.

TikToker 3: According to the National Endowment for the Arts, fewer than half of American adults reported having read any book in 2022.

Horowitch: So I do wonder if we will get to a point where we’re not even reading more text, because it’s just videos now.

TikToker 4: I’m about to talk about the death of reading on the app that is allegedly killing it. Stay with me.

Rosin: Yeah, there was a really interesting interview with one of the leading figures at The New York Times who talked about the Times shifting very explicitly towards video, and that seemed like a moment. Partly just because we’re journalists, but we do think of The New York Times as a bastion of sort of literary, straight newsgathering. So it’s something interesting. And them acknowledging the future is video, we haven’t really even talked about that. We’ve been pretending as if we’re going to continue to live in a world inundated with text, but that may not be true at all.

I know you interviewed a lot of professors, researchers. Did they have ideas beyond, Woe is me. I wish more people would read books? Do they have method strategies, anything they employ that wasn’t just kind of, Do like we used to do?

Horowitch: Well, I think there are some signs that there’s a little bit of a backlash brewing. Certainly, we see it against tech in schools, you know, states banning cellphones during the school day. And when Texas did so, a district in Dallas saw 200,000 more library books checked out. So I think that—

Rosin: Wait—explain the experiment again. What exactly happened?

Horowitch: So Texas banned cellphones during the school day, and nearly two dozen states did it. And then the year after that happened, there were 200,000 more library books checked out at this Dallas school district than there had been the year before. So it did seem that I guess when people had more time and there was nothing else they could do, they were like, Okay, I’ll read a book.

Rosin: That actually seems very simple and significant ’cause so many schools are banning cellphones during the day that it’s just out of sheer boredom. You’re like, Okay, fine, I’ll read a book. My teacher says it’s interesting. I’ll try it out.

Horowitch: Right. Exactly. And so we don’t know that it inherently leads to better grades or people doing better in schools. It seems like the evidence is mixed there. But it does seem like it definitely forces people to just, Okay, we have so much more time back, so either we’re gonna hang out with each other, we’re gonna get a book, maybe do all of the above. So that’s a change. But in terms of things that we can do to kind of address this, I guess the challenge is, because I think it is such a big shift, it would require something of similar magnitude to move back to reading if that’s something that we decide is worth it and something we’re not willing to give up.

And here I think it’s useful to return to how I opened the essay, which is with this metaphor about the Library of Alexandria. I wrote about that because the Library of Alexandria is known as this real intellectual center of this earlier era. And for a long time, people thought that the library was destroyed by an act of war, that Julius Caesar burned it. What the new consensus is among historians is that: No, these external attacks didn’t matter that much. The library died because people didn’t care that it lived. They neglected it, and then it eventually fell into disrepair. And so I think that is a really useful way to think about the present, that we just have to decide because we’re doing this to ourselves, that we want to choose differently.

Rosin: Did doing this research change your own reading habits at all?

[Music]

Horowitch: I definitely have tried to read more. And I think what I find is just when I do have time, I always love reading and get back into it. But there’s always that little first hump that I have to get over, to kind of retrain my brain. I don’t know if that’s actually what’s happening inside my brain, but it feels like I can’t just get through it, and then I start to get engrossed, and then I stop looking at my phone as much. And so it’s definitely made me think about that much more and notice it more clearly.

Rosin: I feel like that’s a no. (Laughs.)

Horowitch: (Laughs.) I’m trying to be positive. I’m like, No, I should read more.

Rosin: Shouldn’t we all.

Well, Rose, thank you so much for joining me today.

Horowitch: Thank you for having me.

Rosin: This episode of Radio Atlantic was produced by Jinae West. It was edited by Kevin Townsend. Yvonne Kim fact-checked. Rob Smierciak engineered and provided original music. We also had music from Breakmaster Cylinder. Claudine Ebeid is the executive producer of Atlantic audio, and Andrea Valdez is our managing editor.

Listeners, if you enjoy the show, you can support our work and the work of all Atlantic journalists when you subscribe to The Atlantic at TheAtlantic.com/Listener.

I’m Hanna Rosin. Thank you for listening.

entries.title: Music’s New Superstar Is Here—And She’s Rather Relaxed
entries.summary: Ella Langley’s country music offers an appealing commodity for this moment: moderation.
entries.content:
Ella Langley’s country music offers an appealing commodity for this moment: moderation.

Whitney Houston and Mariah Carey are two of music’s great masters of emotional excess, and it’s long seemed fitting that they’ve shared one of Billboard’s highest achievements: the longest-running Hot 100 No. 1 for a solo woman. That record was set over 14 weeks by Houston’s “I Will Always Love You” in 1992 and 1993, and then matched by Carey’s “We Belong Together” in 2005. (We’re talking about non-holiday tracks; the 22-week reign of Carey’s “All I Want for Christmas Is You” is in a class of its own.) The enduring dominance of songs by these two volcanoes of vulnerability seemed to prove an iron law of pop: Greatness comes from giving all of oneself.

Their record has now been equaled by the 27-year-old country singer Ella Langley, a very different artist indeed. The Alabama-born Langley has been rising in country music for a couple of years now, but her second album, this year’s Dandelion, has leapt the genre’s fences to reach a general audience. Three Langley tracks are in the top 10 of the Billboard Hot 100. The single “Choosin’ Texas” may soon notch its 15th week at No. 1, breaking Carey and Houston’s records. Where those women belted loudly, Langley offers moderation—an appealing commodity in this cultural moment.

Understanding that moment means understanding how the meaning of the Billboard charts has changed since Houston and Carey’s days: Thanks to streaming, what matters is how often a song is played, not only how many copies it sells. That shift has helped country music—which generally seeks to weave itself into everyday life rather than stick out through spectacle—become the popular sound of the 2020s. When Morgan Wallen’s “Last Night” hit No. 1 on the Hot 100 in 2023, it was the first country song to do so since 2005 (unless you count edge cases such as Taylor Swift’s 10-minute “All Too Well,” which charted in the country world despite sounding more like pop folk, and Lil Nas X’s “Old Town Road,” a source of categorization controversy). Since then, 10 other country tracks have made it to the top.

Out of all the arrivals to Nashville’s gold rush, Langley doesn’t seem like an obvious candidate for success. Her voice tends to stay in a middle range, with only a slight hint of huskiness. Her arrangements aim for modern classicism, placing familiar instruments—guitars, mandolins, Wurlitzer pianos—in polished productions that forgo twists or anthemic buildups. Her tattoos and brunette bangs hint at a bohemian persona, but her glossy-looking album covers and easygoing interview responses aren’t very edgy. To hear what’s unusual about her, you have to listen closely, and pick up on what she’s not saying.

“Choosin’ Texas” is a breezy, sad track that sounds like an even gentler version of the Eagles. The narrator is from Tennessee; her man is from Texas; Langley realizes he’s leaving her for a girl from his home state. The details are precisely chosen clichés: She’s tipped off by “the way he’s two-steppin’ round the room”; in a lovely, pedal-steel-drenched second verse, she muses that he must’ve “forgot about the Smoky Mountain rain.” She doesn’t sound mad, or even annoyed, by this betrayal. Because she knows she “wasn’t a match” for the spark of a Texas girl, she “shoulda known better than to take him back to Abilene.” She’s soberly analyzing her defeat—and trying to teach herself a lesson.

Most of Langley’s work features that kind of wry self-talk. Her hit “I Can’t Love You Anymore,” a collaboration with Wallen, is like a lecture from her brain to her heart: “What part of over don’t I understand?” she chides herself when she gets to daydreaming about an ex. On “Weren’t for the Wind,” a single from her 2024 debut, Hungover, she mixes and matches grammatical tenses to suit the layers of her own flakiness: “I’d make a promise but I know later / I’m bound to change my mind.”

Introspection is hardly foreign to country music, but Langley’s coolly reflective approach stands out in a genre defined by social collisions—by scenes of cowboys and cowgirls settling scores. Langley’s mentor Miranda Lambert, a co-writer on “Choosin’ Texas” and an executive producer for Dandelion, broke out in the 2000s with scorching revenge anthems such as “Kerosene” and “Gunpowder & Lead.” Wallen specializes in whiskey-drunk seductions to skeptical girls who can’t resist his charms. The inevitability that underlies many of Langley’s songs recalls “I Can’t Make You Love Me” by the Americana legend Bonnie Raitt, but in the place of Raitt’s devastation, Langley shrugs with acceptance.

One of Langley’s best—and most telling—songs is another current smash, “Be Her.” Over a groove punctuated by polite handclaps, Langley airs her jealousy about a woman who “drinks wine by the glass, not by the bottle,” who “knows being rich is just a state of mind,” and who “stays talkin’ to Jesus.” In other words, she’s fascinated by a good girl—one who’s internalized the advice of her parents, the Bible, and definitely some wellness podcasts. Although the reference points here are Christian, the outlook scans a bit Buddhist: The song’s subject doesn’t desire “much of anything.” Perhaps the person Langley wants to be is not someone else but rather a dream of her own enlightened future self.

The song is a perfectly Gen Z take on “Jolene”—Dolly Parton’s country classic of female jealousy—in that it’s about self-control rather than sex appeal. Striking a balance between mellowness and fun is an imperative for a cohort that’s drinking less, hooking up less, and staying in more. Many other country stars of the moment, including the oft-canceled Wallen and the former drug dealer Jelly Roll, present as sinners struggling for redemption. Langley’s closest sonic predecessor, Kacey Musgraves, has long searched for transcendence—though in more woo-woo terms than Langley does, and with (on Musgraves’s lusty new album) the sort of neediness that often gets written off as cringe.

Langley could never be cringe. She wants love but doesn’t sweat attachment; if her guy is gonna leave her for Texas, she’s not going to siege the Alamo. Partying, sex, adventure—on Dandelion, she treats these less like life’s great temptations and more like small, manageable indulgences. Her music accordingly pares down the signature elements of a genre in which reckless abandon and defiant self-expression have long been central. I think I enjoy Langley’s pale and pleasant songs, but I can barely remember how they sound when I’m not listening. Like nonalcoholic beer and an early bedtime, she’s a delicacy for the overcrowded now.

entries.title: The New Chilling Effect
entries.summary: Too many institutions are teaching young people to stay quiet.
entries.content:
If you’re looking for a reason to despair over the state of American education, there are many places you could start. You could, for example, worry about the all-consuming phones—or the gambling and TikTok addictions that come with them. You could worry about the free fall in literacy, the decline in math fluency, the rise of AI, the insanity of college-obsessed parents, the sharp increase in truancy, the teen mental-health crisis, and on and on.

Yet something that deserves more attention is damage to free expression—not in the classroom but in the public sphere of high school: the realms of student newspapers, student government, and other aspects of everyday life. School is meant to prepare students for the real world, but too many institutions are training kids to be silent.

The three of us are the editors of our high-school student newspaper in Washington, D.C., with a staff of about 60 student journalists. We ask a lot of questions. We want to know when and why things happened. We cover big policy changes (a ban on phones) and small schedule changes (an earlier start date to the school year). We cover all kinds of controversies—among them, senior pranks, changes in faculty, and debates about assemblies and theatrical productions. Writers argue in our opinion section about calculator theft, the cure for teenage sleep deprivation, grade inflation, AI policies, and more.

Although many teachers at our school agree to interviews, some consistently decline them. They tell us they’re afraid that saying the wrong thing will result in a stern lecture from a boss or come up in a future job interview. They say they do not want their views at a specific moment in time to be permanently findable on the internet. That’s their right, of course. And we could use more people in society who know that they don’t have to opine on everything just because they can. But we ask teachers for interviews because they are experts who often have more information about the inner workings of the school than we do. We want our publication to be like a small-town newspaper, one that gives its readers a comprehensive view of how our community thinks and feels, and one that advances the cause of seeking the truth and reporting it. But our readers cannot get a full view of the community when people are too afraid to talk.

And people are definitely afraid. This problem, as we see it, is much bigger than our school newspaper. The era of social media came with the promise of hyper-democratization—no longer do you need to be an established pundit to get your message out. (All of us, born in the 2000s, struggle to imagine it any other way.) Although there is much to celebrate about the ways in which the internet has connected us, the permanence of the internet has given rise to censorship pressures—both individually and institutionally. People are so nervous about saying the wrong thing that they choose to say nothing.

Taken to the extreme, a culture of silence can be extraordinarily harmful. As a society, we have to accept that people’s opinions change over time. We can start by treating high school as a time for students to take intellectual risks. In this phase of life, students discover their interests and form their opinions. They try out for sports teams and join clubs and meet new people. And through that experimentation, they come into themselves and grow more independent. The adults who care about students—parents, teachers, school administrators, prospective employers—should encourage them to speak up, to debate, and, yes, even to fail. And they should do so without stepping in to intervene at every turn.

Recently, the Montgomery County, Maryland, school district made local headlines after officials required administrators to review student newspapers prior to publication and outright censor certain reports based on vague standards. School officials tend to approach such matters with varying degrees of subtlety: One administrator at our school floated the idea of a prior-review system for our articles and instructed us on how we should contact sources, while another told us our decision to publish an article about them was reprehensible.

Administrators owe it to their students to support a culture of free expression, and high schoolers need to keep contributing to that culture by saying what they think. Free expression is a principle that seems easy to uphold in theory, but requires some degree of courage in practice. To start, we must reaffirm that our individual and collective right to free expression transcends the substance of anything that any one of us might say. A robust—and sometimes messy—marketplace of ideas is in the best interest of all of us.

In Des Moines, Iowa, in 1965, five students wore black armbands emblazoned with peace symbols to school in protest of the U.S.’s escalating involvement in the Vietnam War. Local high-school administrators suspended three of the five students. The school district soon found itself justifying the suspensions in a three-year legal battle that ended with a Supreme Court victory for the students in Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District.

The Des Moines administrators said they blocked the armbands because of the possibility that the silent protest could create a disruption, even though no disruption occurred. If a police officer were to prohibit a person on the street from wearing a black armband because that person could hypothetically incite a large protest, any reasonable American would rightly see the officer’s actions as a violation of the First Amendment. The same logic should apply across society.

Of course, creating a culture of openness and respectful debate requires more than staving off government interference. If large groups of people decide that silence is safer than risking disagreement, and if they instruct children to be silent in turn, our fundamental right to free expression will crumble from within.

entries.title: The Other Victims of a Wrongful Conviction
entries.summary: For years, I reported on one man’s incarceration for a crime he didn’t commit—and ignored the stories of a grieving family who thought they had gotten justice.
entries.content:
For years, I reported on one man’s incarceration for a crime he didn't commit—and ignored the stories of a grieving family who thought they had gotten justice.

“I bet you’re extremely happy,” Jay Young texted me. “So you and the slimy media going to fawn over his side and forget about my dad’s family and friends now?”

It was March 10, 2021, and Benjamine Spencer, the man serving a life sentence for murdering Young’s father, Jeffrey, was about to be freed. The state of Texas had concluded that 34 years before, it had almost surely convicted an innocent man; a Dallas judge was expected to rule in Spencer’s favor any day. Young held me partly responsible for this turn of events. My reporting for The Atlantic had unearthed exculpatory evidence that helped bring about Spencer’s release.

I texted back, offering to talk. He demurred. For three years, we barely spoke.

Then, in August, 2024, a few days after the publication of my book Bringing Ben Home, Young sent me a congratulatory message. “I have zero ill will or negative feelings toward you or anything or anyone,” he wrote. “I would really appreciate a conversation.”

I’d rather not, I thought. Young distrusted the media. Years earlier, he had reluctantly granted me an interview for my Atlantic story. Our interactions since then had been prickly; I didn’t relish starting a new round.

But something about his words—the psychological distance between his two texts—snagged my attention. What had happened in those three-plus years to turn his anger into warmth? Over time, from dozens of conversations with Spencer, I had developed a fairly detailed map of the inmate’s legal, emotional, and spiritual journey. But Jay Young? I knew almost nothing about him.

Every time an innocent person is convicted and later exonerated for a violent crime—something that has occurred more than 2,800 times in the past four decades in America—there are two sets of victims, each suffering a unique torture. The innocent prisoner loses years of freedom, while his children, spouse, parents, siblings, and friends are left with a jagged hole in their lives. But on the other side, the people devastated by a violent crime—the victim (if he survives), his family and friends—also suffer years of chaos as the exoneration plays out. In many cases, no one is brought to account in the end. When an innocent man is freed, the stories of those victims tend to go overlooked.

In this regard, I had failed Jay and the other members of Jeffrey Young’s family. I would try to fix that. Over the next 18 months, Jay Young and I spoke repeatedly, and I came to see that the legal system had thrown him, too, into a kind of prison. He had watched helplessly as the case against his father’s alleged killer had crumbled and Spencer walked out of prison to thunderous applause. I had not registered Young’s solitary, emotionally treacherous journey—one marked by suicidality, loneliness, and addiction. If I had, I would have discovered that Ben Spencer and Jay Young, two men on opposite sides of a murder, had both been betrayed by the system that was supposed to protect them.

Jeffrey Young was working late at his office in a warehouse district of Dallas on March 22, 1987, a Sunday night. The acting president of a clothing-import company, he was white, 33 years old, married to his high-school sweetheart, and the father of three preteens. There were no security cameras to capture what happened that night, but police later theorized that two men waited in the empty parking lot. When Young walked out of his office, they tackled him, stole his cash and jewelry, struck him on the head, and cracked his skull in five places. They placed Young in the back seat or trunk of his BMW and drove over the Trinity River into West Dallas, a poor Black neighborhood reeling from the relentless violence of the crack epidemic. Somehow, Young managed to escape the car, falling out onto the street, where he lay bleeding and shaking as a crowd gathered. Spooked, the perpetrators abandoned the BMW in a nearby alley and ran away. Young died at Parkland Memorial Hospital at 3:05 a.m.

The randomness of Young’s murder terrified white Dallas, and it spurred Ross Perot, a billionaire and soon-to-be presidential candidate, to offer a reward through his company: $25,000 for evidence leading to the arrest and indictment of Jeffrey Young’s killers—a sizable sum in 1987. Four days after the assault, police detained Benjamine Spencer. He was Black, 22 years old, newly married with a baby on the way. He had no record of violence.

Jay Young, who was 12 years old when his father was killed, recalls greeting the news with ambivalence. “I felt like, I trust law enforcement and they found the right person. But I remember thinking: That’s great, but it’s not going to bring my dad back,” he told me in 2017.

Over the seven months after the murder, police conducted an investigation that, with the clarity of hindsight, resembled a checklist of how to convict an innocent man. No physical evidence connected Spencer to the crime. The evidence consisted of the testimony of three neighbors who sought to claim Perot’s $25,000 reward, and of a jailhouse informant who sought a more lenient sentence. Facing up to 25 years, the informant walked out of prison two months after his testimony. Finally, police ignored a far more plausible suspect. At trial, the prosecutor said nothing as at least two of his four witnesses lied under oath, and he hid evidence that would have discredited the state’s star witness. The resulting guilty verdict was thrown out, but after a second trial, Spencer was convicted and sentenced to life in prison on March 28, 1988.

The murder robbed Jay Young of his hero and the family of its financial and emotional stability. Greg Ward, who was Young’s best friend in ninth grade, recalls a home in perpetual gloom, the family burdened by “sadness, confusion, desperation.” Young could usually bury his pain: He was handsome and popular, always had a girlfriend, and played varsity sports. “But he had fits of rage and anger,” Ward told me.

The police and prosecutors cannot be blamed for the first tragedy—that fault lies with the murderers alone—but the second tragedy was their doing. The damage to Spencer, and to the Youngs, would not surface until the case against Spencer began to unravel.

Thirteen years passed. On May 20, 2001, Jim McCloskey sat in the visitors’ room at Coffield Unit, a maximum-security prison 100 miles south of Dallas. McCloskey’s organization, Centurion Ministries, was the first national organization to reinvestigate dubious convictions, and it had a near-perfect win record. He told Spencer they were taking his case.

McCloskey quickly found problems with the conviction: the monetary incentive for the eyewitnesses, the informant’s lies, the police failure to investigate a more viable suspect, the optical impossibility of identifying anyone running from the car on that dark night. He offered the findings to The Dallas Morning News, which ran stories and editorials detailing the flaws. Suddenly, Young recalled, his father’s murder was everywhere—the regular subject of media coverage, court proceedings, and public protests. “I said, Really? Is this going to be the rest of my life?”

Prosecutors blocked Spencer’s path at every turn. When McCloskey and Cheryl Wattley, Spencer’s new attorney, asked the district attorney’s office to look at their evidence, the DA declined. When they filed a writ of habeas corpus with a Dallas judge, requesting a hearing to consider McCloskey’s new evidence, the prosecutors fought it. They assured the Young family that legal challenges and media coverage were typical (they were not) and told them not to worry, because the conviction was “ironclad.”

For the Youngs, every article, every court filing, every parole hearing dragged them back to the day their father died. Determined not to let the prisoner they held responsible go free, they waged their own campaign to keep Spencer in prison.

Doing so was exhausting. “I don’t think that a lot of people actually understand the thought process and the emotions that you have to go through,” Jimmy Young, Jay’s younger brother, told me in 2017—fielding calls from journalists, organizing letter campaigns against Spencer’s parole, meeting to strategize their arguments before the parole board. “We have to go to parole hearings every year. Every year. And the reason why we all do that is because we believe in the justice system” and, he added, “we believe that you should pay for what you did.”

Inside Coffield, Spencer was paying for something he did not do. But innocent prisoners, who deserve to be angry, can’t afford the luxury. Spencer believed that bitterness would destroy his soul. It was a “cancer,” he said, and he trained his mind to eradicate the smallest traces. He prayed for the neighbors who framed him. He clung to the hope that one day he would be cleared. Most of all, he thought about the Young family.

“My heart truly goes out to them, for they have suffered the greatest loss,” he wrote his then-ex-wife, Debra. (He and Debra remarried in 2022.) Later, when the family opposed his appeals and his parole, he wrote, “I understand their position … They trusted the authorities to find and prosecute the person(s) responsible.”

Over the objections of the district attorney, Dallas County District Judge Rick Magnis held an evidentiary hearing in July 2007. Jay Young, then 32, sat in the packed courtroom behind the state’s appellate lawyers. The hearing was a “circus,” he fumed in 2020, recalling that day, and “Magnis was the ringleader.” The judge took over the questioning at times, grilling the eyewitnesses and the lead homicide detective, openly skeptical of their testimony. “It was a joke in my opinion.”

But as Young watched the proceedings, doubt stole into his mind, he told me nearly a decade later. The lawyers for the DA’s office acted “lackadaisical,” he said, texting on their phones and failing to rebut Spencer’s arguments or rehabilitate their own discredited witnesses. “I remember sitting there going, Something’s not right,” Young recently told me. “But I just never really wanted to open those wounds.”

Judge Magnis found that Spencer deserved a new trial based on “actual innocence,” and recommended that the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals allow a new trial to proceed. But the district attorney opposed the judge’s finding and repeatedly assured the Youngs that the decision would be overturned.

In 2010, while both sides waited for the court to weigh in, prosecutors reinterviewed the state’s star witness, Gladys Oliver. She told them that she had received $5,000 to $10,000 in reward money in exchange for her testimony. She had almost certainly lied about it at trial, which was grounds for vacating the conviction. But instead of turning over the damning evidence to Spencer’s attorneys or the court, the prosecutors placed the interview notes in a file cabinet and closed the drawer.

Two prosecutors later told me they had, at the time, believed that the interview wasn’t relevant. This defies belief: Evidence undermining the credibility of the state’s most convincing witness could have swayed the judges still considering Spencer’s case. But the judges never saw the notes. In 2011, the Court of Criminal Appeals rejected Magnis’s finding and ruled unanimously that Spencer would serve out his life sentence.

The decision devastated Spencer, who had believed he was about to be freed. “I don’t want you to worry about me, but I’m really struggling right now,” he wrote Debra shortly after the ruling. “I keep wondering: Is prison the life that my future holds for me?” But he quickly rebounded. “I knew that I didn’t commit this offense and God knew the truth about the offense, and in that I had freedom,” he later explained to me. “I’ve always been free, even though I was incarcerated.”

Jay Young felt no victory, only dread. “I just remember thinking, This isn’t over,” he told me in 2017. It was the latest move in a game that would continue with parole hearings, newspaper stories, rallies, and the prospect that the next elected district attorney might open a new investigation. Spencer’s case had become the cacophonous soundtrack of his life.

In those years, Young faced his darkest period. His career derailed. His marriage ended in 2014. He had custody of his two children half the time; the other half, alone in his home, he began to drink too much.

It’s impossible to calculate how much of Young’s trouble sprung from the trauma of losing his dad, and how much arose from the disabling chaos that erupted every time his father’s murder resurfaced in the courts and the media. “He was lost and he was in trouble,” his friend Greg Ward remembered. “Jay is one of the smartest people I know. Couldn’t keep a job. Couldn’t keep a family. Couldn’t keep things running because he was always running himself.”

“I was in a prison of depression and anger,” Young reflected when we talked earlier this year. “It kind of took my life.”

Spencer, incarcerated for life, felt free. Young, who could come and go as he pleased, was shackled to a trauma that never seemed to end.

Young’s prediction that the case would resurface came true in 2017, when I arrived in Dallas on assignment for The Atlantic. Along with Daryl Parker, a private investigator who believed that Spencer was innocent, I spent weeks canvassing down-and-out parts of Dallas, trying to dig up evidence 30 years after the crime. We found one “eyewitness” who admitted that he had accused Spencer for the reward money; later, he signed an affidavit and passed a polygraph stating he had lied at trial. We found the jailhouse informant, who casually told us that he had made up Spencer’s confession to get a shorter sentence. We found a new alibi witness who insisted that Spencer had been at his home at the time of the assault. Because the second “eyewitness” was dead, the conviction now rested entirely on the testimony of the star witness, Gladys Oliver, who refused to talk with us.

The Atlantic article ran in the winter of 2018. Soon thereafter, Spencer’s defense team asked John Creuzot, the newly elected district attorney, to reopen the case. Cruezot’s prosecutors confirmed our evidence and found more: the notes from the 2010 interview with Gladys Oliver, tucked away in a file cabinet, showing that she, too, had perjured herself in court. “We’ve had it all along,” Creuzot told me. “We just weren’t honest about it.”

In January 2021, the DA’s office joined Spencer’s attorneys in court to argue that Spencer had not received a fair trial and should be released while the state decided whether to retry him or dismiss the charges. The judge agreed, and on March 12, 2021, Ben Spencer walked out of the Dallas jail.

Outside, a crowd of supporters had swelled to more than 200 and, against security protocol, the guards opened the doors to the lobby of the jail. Supporters surged in, laughing, singing, high-fiving. Spencer and Debra walked from the jail into the lobby and halted, dumbstruck, as people started cheering. They inched forward, holding hands, smiling. The sea of people parted as they walked out of the building into the warm March evening.

Jay Young learned that Spencer was free hours later, when he received a text from a reporter seeking a comment. In the coverage, Jeffrey Young was barely mentioned, and then only to set up Spencer’s triumph over the system. Jay Young was justified when he sent me his text: So you and the slimy media going to fawn over his side and forget about my dad’s family and friends now?

“That person is dead,” Young said, when I brought up the text earlier this year. “I’m a different person.”

Around the time he sent me that message five years ago, Young had stopped drinking and was wrestling with emotions that he had for years anesthetized with alcohol. “I was slowly falling down into the pit,” he later told me about that time. Over the next three years, he threw himself into a 12-step program and his church as if his life depended on them—which it did.

On May 15, 2024, the Court of Criminal Appeals ruled that Spencer had not received a fair trial. This allowed District Attorney Creuzot to decide whether to exonerate him. Over the summer, Creuzot and his lead prosecutor, Cynthia Garza, met once with several members of the Young family and once with Jay alone. They told Young that they planned to exonerate Spencer, but they did not explain their reasoning. “It’s like my dad died again,” Young reflected afterward, in one of the many conversations we have had this year. “I felt very disrespected, like they didn’t care and somehow [it was] my fault.” Young had called Creuzot a “coward,” bringing the meeting to an unhappy end.

It struck me as unfair to take these feelings out on the current DA. Creuzot and his prosecutors did not convict an innocent person; they were correcting their predecessors’ mistakes. Young reached out to Jennifer Thompson—a woman who famously identified an innocent man as her rapist, and who personally understands the damage to victims on both sides of a wrongful conviction—for guidance. In her view, the Young family had every reason to be angry. The system had failed them in 1987, and it tried to cover its mistakes for three decades. The new prosecutor kept the Youngs in the dark until the last minute and presented Spencer’s exoneration as a fait accompli. Doing so “re-traumatized them,” Thompson told me. “You’re leaving them with all the questions and none of the answers.”

Thompson, who founded Healing Justice, an organization dedicated to helping victims of violence and people who were wrongly convicted navigate toward reconciliation, said that anyone in the Young family’s position would struggle to know who or what to believe. Dallas prosecutors told them one thing for 30 years, Thompson said, “and then the system is like, Just kidding!”

On August 29, 2024, more than 150 of Spencer’s supporters, double the room’s listed capacity, packed into the 283rd District Court in Dallas. Thirty-seven years ago, Spencer had been convicted for murder in this very court. Now he would learn whether he would be absolved. Wedged into a crowded bench, I heard my phone ping. “I’m here,” the text said, and standing at the door was Jay Young, looking shell-shocked as he scanned the room.

The crowd hushed as Garza, the prosecutor, announced that Spencer should be exonerated based on actual innocence. Soon Spencer—tall, lanky, and elegant in his blue pinstripe suit—stood up; he was visibly nervous. Unaware that the victim’s son sat 30 feet away, he spoke for less than two minutes, chiefly to point out that he and his family were not the only ones who had been harmed by the system. “The Young family were victims as well, because they lost a loved one, and the person who was actually responsible for what happened to Jeffrey Young was not brought to justice,” he said. “So I’d ask that you pray for the Young family and be mindful of them. I don’t want them to be forgotten.” Young stared at his feet, betraying no emotion.

Afterward, in the hallway, I asked Young, “What do you think?” “I think my body would have told me if I was in the presence of my dad’s murderer,” he murmured. “It didn’t.”

Let’s survey the landscape from Jay Young’s perspective as it looked on that morning in August 2024. His father’s murder was now a 37-year-old cold case. There was no DNA, the witnesses were scattered or dead, and barring a confession from the killer, his murder would most likely never be solved. Any “closure”—if there is such a thing—had evaporated. Ripping off a Band-Aid didn’t begin to capture the pain, Young said. “It was like ripping a freaking scar that’s already been healed.”

He understands the importance of making things right for Spencer, who can never recover those years. When the detectives took him in for questioning, he was 22, newly married and starting a family; he walked out a 56-year-old man with gray hair and a grown son. The Youngs’ loss, not to mention the emotional upheaval they experienced because of three decades of prosecutorial intransigence, was to the outside world a footnote in Spencer’s story. “We just felt forgotten,” Young said. “Ben is a victim. But we lost a life.”

Young recognized, for perhaps the first time, that the judicial system is a zero-sum game. For years, Spencer was the loser, but one day in August 2024 the prosecutor flipped a switch, and the Youngs were left with less than nothing. Young was forced to reframe his entire story. The man he had hated for decades and whose parole his family had thwarted year after year was now deemed faultless. “Everything changed in my life all at once,” Young said. “And I was tired of fighting.”

Three weeks after Spencer’s exoneration, Young was alone in his house in Frisco, just north of Dallas, ruminating on his life. He found it unbearably bleak: 50 years old, divorced, isolated from his family, his career off track. His girlfriend was gone, and so was the one constant in his life: his belief that his dad’s killer had been caught and punished.

“I remember hearing these voices in my head saying, Just go end your life. And I said, You know what? Okay.” He walked toward the closet in his bedroom, where he stored three guns. He saw his reflection in the mirror. What am I doing? he thought. He called his mentor in his 12-step program. “I need someone to come over here and take my guns,” he said. He was going for a walk; he’d leave the front door unlocked.

It was noon and the neighborhood streets were empty when Young, emotionally spent, collapsed on the sidewalk. “I remember how hot it was because I took my shirt off and I can feel the concrete. I remember looking up, and I said, ‘God, I cannot do this anymore. I cannot do this.’” He made his way home, checked that the guns were gone, and fell into an exhausted sleep early that night.

He awakened confused by an unfamiliar emotion: peace. The interior clamor had stilled. “I cannot explain it. That’s how it feels to wake up the next day after surrendering.” He marvels that none of his concerns—his separation from his girlfriend, unhappiness with his work, worry over his finances—had evaporated. This peace was untethered to his circumstances. “I was just happy.”

Young serves as a greeter at Hope Fellowship, a Texas megachurch with more than 7,000 attendees across four campuses. Over coffee one day in early 2025, Young told Dustin Chappell, one of the pastors, that when he was a child, his father had been murdered—and the man convicted for it had recently been exonerated. Chappell was floored: He had just met a man in church who had also been exonerated. Some weeks later, Chappell sat down with Young and Charles Chatman, who had spent 27 years in prison for rape before DNA evidence cleared him.

When Young began talking about Ben Spencer, Chappell later recalled, “Charles happens to mention, ‘Yeah, I know Ben.’ And Jay’s like, ‘Wait, what?’ And Charles said, ‘Yeah, I was in prison with Ben.’”

Chatman did not know Spencer well at Coffield Unit, but he knew that Spencer’s behavior was not the behavior of a guilty man. He didn’t hustle, didn’t smuggle in cigarettes or other contraband and sell them at a premium. “Those [innocent] guys have a special way, a different way of thinking,” Chatman told me.

After Chatman was exonerated in 2008, he visited Spencer in the Dallas jail where he was staying during his evidentiary hearing. “I ask him, ‘Man to man, face to face: Did you do that crime?’ When he told me, face to face, ‘Charles, man, I did not do this crime,’ looking in his eyes, I decided then that I would be behind him.”

Seventeen years later, at a Starbucks near the church, Young asked Chatman if he thought Spencer was innocent, and Chatman said that he did. “Jay’s whole countenance changes,” Chappell recalled. “He says to Charles, ‘You know, I’d really like to meet Ben.’”

When I heard this story, I thought about the only time I had seen Spencer near tears. Here’s a man who had spent three decades in a maximum-security prison in Texas, who had survived gang wars and lockdowns and 100-degree heat with no air-conditioning. I never heard him complain. One day in June 2021, three months after Spencer’s release, we were eating sandwiches on a shady bench outside the Hall Arts Hotel, where he worked as a facilities engineer. I asked him what he most wanted in life, now that he was free.

“I know that my greatest wish may never come true, but I continue to pray and hope for it.” He cleared his throat quietly, and I could see that he was choking back tears. “My wish is that one day the Young family could just come to accept the truth—the fact that I’m innocent.”

And I thought: That will never happen.

It happened this way. Soon after his conversation with Young, Chatman told Spencer that Young had asked to meet with him. He expected Spencer to demur—why would he want to unearth his painful past? But Spencer told him he’d drive up to Hope Fellowship the first Sunday he could. On March 2, 2025, in the church’s overflow room, Spencer met Chatman, who texted Young, and a few minutes later, Young walked out of the sanctuary. “Jay looked at me and looked at Spencer, just with a big smile on his face,” he told me.

“Then he put his hand out to shake hands, and it was like slow motion,” Young recalled, picking up the story when we talked a few days later. “That was when it locked it up 100 percent. Once I shook his hand, that’s when everything made sense. Like, you read a book and you don’t know what’s going to happen. Why is this happening? And then you get to the last chapter and you just go: I get it.”

As all three later recounted to me, they spoke for an hour and then decided to find a restaurant for lunch. Nervously at first, but then more intimately, they talked about their families—Spencer’s newly reunited family and Young’s newly divided one. They talked about the Bible. Young described his struggle with alcohol and depression. “It was weird because it was like I had known this guy forever,” Young told me. “Because I have.”

Finally, Young broached the question that mattered most. “I have to ask this because I want to hear it from your mouth and not from newspapers or other people. But me asking you, this isn’t accusing you. I just want to hear you say you didn’t do it.’

“I didn’t,” Spencer promised.

“Can you forgive me for going to those parole hearings?” Young asked.

“Man, you only were going by what you were told,” Spencer said, laughing. “I would have done the same thing.”

The Christian Church, Pastor Chappell reflected, has spent 2,000 years preaching about grace and forgiveness, but to see people live out those qualities—well, that takes his breath away. “In a world that is full of chaos and hatred and polarization, to see unity where the world says unity shouldn’t exist, I think is nothing short of beautiful,” he said in wonder.

Put aside the Christian language for a moment. My sense, having watched the ticktock of this unlikely reconciliation, is that the story transcends religion. It speaks to a uniquely human capacity, for two people to lift up their eyes from their grievances to find a more expansive landscape, to pursue an uncomfortable truth even when it robs them of family solidarity and the sweet taste of vindictiveness, to understand “the enemy” and recognize he is nothing of the sort.

“I had freedom that I’d never felt before,” Young told me recently. “In my life. About anything.”

That afternoon, I received a text from Spencer: “Guess who I’m having lunch with?” In the photo, Spencer is wearing a tan suit with a plaid tie and a tie clip. Young is wearing a Team Hope sweatshirt and blue jeans. Both men are beaming.

At home that night, Young was studying photos from their lunch: Spencer’s broad smile, his arm around Young’s shoulders, his body relaxed. “I could tell how happy he was,” Young said. “And I thought: He gets to start his life over. And I get to start my life over too.”

Five months later, Spencer’s friends and family packed into a restaurant in downtown Dallas to celebrate Spencer’s one-year anniversary as an exonerated man. People key to Spencer’s exoneration—Judge Rick Magnis, Spencer’s two lawyers, and Daryl Parker, the investigator who worked with me to find exculpatory evidence—chatted quietly in the corner until the toasts began. Spencer glanced toward the door, then suddenly bounded across the room and lunged at a man, almost knocking him over in a hug.

“This is Jay Young, Jeffrey Young’s son,” Spencer announced. The room hushed. After a beat, everyone stood and began to clap until the place pounded with emotion. Judge Magnis strode over and shook Young’s hand: “Really happy for you, and proud.” Cheryl Wattley, Spencer’s longtime attorney, did the same. Young looked from the judge and to the attorney, his onetime nemeses. “Good work. Good for y’all,” he said. But it was Debra and Ben’s friends and family who moved him. They stood in line to thank him, tongue-tied; some were crying.

The next morning, Young sent Debra a text. “I felt like I was with family/friends. You guys are inspiring. Love you guys.” Debra responded: “I can’t tell you how many people have asked about you already this morning. We love you!”

You can’t choose your family, but you can choose your tribe. True, tribes can be anchored around DNA, ethnicity, religion, or politics. But they can also cohere around a set of shared values: here, the search for truth and the hunger for freedom, whether physical or emotional. Jay Young and Ben Spencer chose each other. “We’re not supposed to be friends,” Young reflected recently, laughing. “You probably have better odds of winning the lottery than this happening.”

How is it that Young and Spencer can sit side by side, so different in fundamental ways: white and Black; raised middle-class and poor; a staunch Republican and a newly registered Democrat; a white-collar businessman and an ex-con; one blamed for the murder of the other’s father?

I don’t claim to be an expert on reconciliation, but allow me to tell you one essential element I observed as their story unfolded: choice. Reconciliation grows out of a series of quiet choices. Spencer and Young were each trying to find a way out of his own subterranean labyrinth, and at every juncture, he had to choose which way to turn.

The choices were counterintuitive, incremental, and always hard. Spencer chose to set aside bitterness, to forgive his enemies, to keep the Youngs’ pain at the forefront of his thought; he refused to break prison rules or hustle for cash or join a gang for his protection. And once he was out, he drove up to Frisco and met one of his main adversaries, a man who helped keep him in prison for an extra 14 years. Young also encountered a series of difficult choices: to listen to his doubts, to put down his final drink, to choose spiritual surrender over suicide, to witness Spencer’s exoneration in a sea of supporters, to meet the man who he once believed had killed his father, and to celebrate that man’s freedom. The two adversaries, whose lives were derailed by a flawed system, have emerged victorious—and friends.

entries.title: The Smithsonian’s Show Trial on Capitol Hill
entries.summary: Republicans grilled a museum head, intensifying Donald Trump’s campaign to reshape the American story.
entries.content:
Republicans grilled a museum head, intensifying Donald Trump’s campaign to reshape the American story.

The nation’s most scrutinized museum director showed up to Congress this week acutely aware of her audience. Anthea Hartig, the head of the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History, referred to the institution as “your” museum. She emphatically affirmed her love of this country. She even cited her family’s Revolutionary War roots. Hartig had been called to testify at two hearings that House Republicans had organized in response to a 162-page White House report accusing the American-history museum of “extreme political activism.” But her opening remarks, in which she also asserted that the report “does not fairly characterize the full body of work of this museum,” didn’t ward off a patriotism sniff test.

“Do you love America?” Representative Eric Burlison of Missouri, a Republican, asked Hartig yesterday during a hearing held by the House Delivering on Government Efficiency Subcommittee. “I love this country unconditionally,” Hartig replied, a sentiment that she would repeat at least half a dozen times during this de facto trial on Capitol Hill.

Whether she confirmed or denied her allegiances didn’t actually matter. Hartig mounted a defense that the museum is “committed to rigorous, fact-based, museum-quality experiences” and acknowledges “enduring debates so that visitors can understand how we’ve wrestled with democracy and citizenship and liberty and national identity.” She called telling the full story of American history “a form of respect for the American people.” But the GOP’s judgment had clearly already been cast. The hearings, rather than serving as opportunities for information gathering, were meant only to widen Donald Trump’s attack on the Smithsonian by enlisting Congress in his efforts to reshape public memory.

Hartig’s appearance followed more than a year of pressure on the Smithsonian from the White House as it has sought to cleanse “improper ideology” from the institution and combat what it sees as a pessimistic approach to history that is too focused on—in Trump’s words—“how bad slavery was.” They marked the most public and outwardly combative test of the institution yet, as some members outright scolded Hartig. Trump’s interest in the Smithsonian has already resulted in changes to wall text discussing his impeachments, and in National Portrait Gallery Director Kim Sajet’s resignation after Trump tried to fire her on social media last year. But the White House has not attempted to overhaul the Smithsonian like it has the Kennedy Center, and it can’t—the institution’s unique legal status and leadership structure insulate it from the president’s whims. By contrast, Congress appropriates much of the public-private institution’s funding and exercises oversight over the institution, which is governed by a board of regents.

Over the course of two days of congressional testimony, Democrats challenged the framing of the museum as politically biased and defended the Smithsonian’s independence, including with their own report, noting errors in the White House document. Republicans, meanwhile, echoed the administration’s claims and dissected Hartig’s public statements, particularly an offhand comment made during a 2021 webinar for the Slave Dwelling Project Conference in which she said, “As we all know in this conference, loving America is very complicated.” The White House had plucked the quote for its report as an example of what it sees as Hartig’s lack of patriotism, and Trump allies in Congress regurgitated it this week like it was her personal slogan, during hearings that quickly took the tenor of a loyalty test.

Perhaps, in a way, it was: The Yale historian David Blight, who also testified both days, noted that in its July 4 report claiming that the museum had strayed from its mission, the Trump administration cited statements dating from the institution’s opening without considering the political context that might have sparked such “triumphalism”: the Cold War. The Heritage Foundation fellow Mike Gonzalez, whom Republicans called as a witness both days, was playing his role in Tuesday’s time warp of a hearing when he leveled accusations of Marxism at the museum before his call to “clean out house” at the Smithsonian. (Witnesses also included the Hillsdale College professor Matthew Spalding yesterday and the president of the conservative think tank American Main Street Initiative, Jeffrey H. Anderson, today.)

Whether “love” is really the metric by which one should assess a historian’s credibility seemed outside the scope of the discussion. Nuance was treated like a threat. It seemed to be no coincidence that the two points that GOP members took issue with most were Hartig’s statement about the complexities of patriotic love and an exhibit that had closed more than three years ago, called “Girlhood (It’s Complicated),” which touched on the struggles of transgender youth.

This allergy to complexity in the representatives’ approach to questions was unmissable. There was a recurring pattern: A GOP member would ask an extremely loaded question—whether the museum should display materials with sexual themes, for instance—and Hartig would try, seemingly in earnest, to respond, and almost instantly she’d be met with: Yes or no! It’s a yes-or-no question! In one particularly illustrative moment today, Representative Mike Carey asked Hartig what she meant when she said she wanted to change the museum’s “America First” approach. She had barely uttered the words “I would argue that the American-history museum” before he cut her off, asserting that she wasn’t answering the question.
